data.title: Fannie And Freddie Likely Secondary Offering Huge For FNMAS
data.description:
Fannie Mae (FNMA) and Freddie Mac (FMCC) were put into conservatorship in 2008 but have been on their path out of conservatorship via retained earnings since 2019. The government currently has a blocking senior equity position that it has successfully defended in court that would need to be restructured if the companies are going to have their balance sheets attract investors in their junior securities, which have had no real economic claim to quarterly earnings since being placed into conservatorship. After papering over their profits with accounting losses from 2008-2011 the government arranged for the net worth sweep in 2012 and took the reversal of these accounting losses in cash to the Treasury for general use to help with budget concerns. That stopped in 2019 when Mark Calabria (FHFA) and Steven Mnuchin (US Treasury) stopped the cash sweep and enabled the companies to retain earnings. It's 2026 now, and we are ripe for admin action. If you look at a vanilla pro forma equity restructuring of their audited balance sheets at historical earnings multiples, my recommendation is junior preferred, like FNMAS.

Investment Thesis

The first Trump administration started the retention of earnings for Fannie and Freddie, but wasn't able to retain enough earnings along with the COVID-19 distraction to really get Fannie and Freddie out of government control along with Mark Calabria's restrictive ERCF. The companies now have been retaining earnings for like 2,348 days, or 6.4 years. Every quarter now they report all-time record net worths' exceeding $170B now combined (Fannie, Freddie). When I look at their balance sheets and project a pro-forma IPO at historical valuations, I convert the government's entire equity position to common shares, their senior preferred stock, and warrants. At a historical valuation, junior preferred shares like FNMAS go to $25 or higher on dividend resumption or conversion to common at terms they agree to. As such, I recommend owning junior preferred shares because, in my view, they have essentially guaranteed security in this pro forma outcome that the entire Trump team has been forecasting and talking about for nearly 10 years, with Mnuchin starting it in 2016 and now it is 2026. Commonly lack anti-dilution protection and thus, in my view, have no security but may be worth more than their current price, but no promises either way there.

New Developments Since My Last Update

I last covered Fannie and Freddie late last year here. As such, I'm only going to cover new developments since then.

Buying Down Mortgage Rates

On February 19, Trump directed Fannie and Freddie to buy down mortgage rates to help ease home affordability:

President Trump directed Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to purchase $200 billion in mortgage-backed securities to further drive down borrowing costs.

Fannie and Freddie historically managed the spread between the 10-Y UST and the 30-Y FRM. Since being placed into conservatorship, they kind of let that spread blow out beyond historical norms, but no one really cared because interest rates were below historical norms during the bulk of the conservatorship. No one really complained that g-fees essentially doubled within conservatorship, so this way of lowering mortgage rates is pretty straightforward. It increases the profits of Fannie and Freddie as well as makes homeownership more affordable. Could they have done this sooner? Yes. Better late than never, though.

Trump Executive Order Directing FHFA

On March 13, Trump gave FHFA a lot of homework with his EO entitled Promoting Access to Mortgage Credit. In section 4, he tasks FHFA with:

revising capital regulations, consistent with appropriate risk-management requirements...

Further, within 120 days, FHFA has to :

submit a report to the Assistant to the President for Economic Policy and the Director of the Office of Management and Budget on the efficiency of national housing finance markets. The report shall identify recommendations for regulatory or legislative changes necessary to address any regulatory or oversight gaps.

How am I interpreting this? It feels like the door is opening to lower capital requirements and codification of housing finance reforms.

FHFA Pulte Key Public Statements Timeline

On January 8, Pulte told CNBC that Trump planned on making a decision on the IPO of Fannie and Freddie in the next month or two.

On February 3, Pulte said in reference to a potential IPO:

We are ready to go when and if the president decides he wants to do that.

When asked about the odds of an IPO this year, Pulte said the odds were "very strong."

On February 6, Pulte said the GSE 'IPO' was ready for a 2.5-5% sale, noting that it wasn't really an IPO because the shares actually already trade, making it technically a secondary offering.

On February 25, Pulte said on Fox News that despite all options being on the table, an IPO is "more likely than not."

That's just this year, previously Pulte had suggested a time frame from November of last year to June of this year. We are in the hot zone.

FHFA's Strategic Plan Smoke Signals

In March, FHFA released their Agency Performance Plan for Fiscal Years 2026/27 which had a few items that I believe are significant (emphasis added with bold):

  1. Strategic Goal 1: Responsibly Oversee Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac for the American People
    1. Subsection 1.2: Ensure the enterprises fulfill all legal and statutory responsibilities
      1. Measure 1.1.3 - U.S. Federal Housing will document its review and approval of each enterprise's quarterly ERCF public disclosure reports before publication. The disclosure reports must be published no later than 10 business days after each quarterly and year-end SEC filing. Documentation will be maintained electronically.
    2. Subsection 1.3: Manage the conservatorships on behalf of the American people
    3. Subsection 3.3: Fulfill statutory reporting requirements

These sections are more important than people realize. These are now performance objectives that only reconcile with one end state, and that is the exit from conservatorship. No other reason to bother with statutory reporting requirements or review the companies' compliance with the ERCF. If you're not exiting conservatorship, these things would never matter. I think this review of ERCF compliance opens the door for the ERCF revisit and rework that, in my view, is necessary for Fannie and Freddie to be able to long-term hit Pulte's $1T valuation that he's spoken about.

FSOC Proposing New Guidelines

The Financial Stability Oversight Council (FSOC) proposed on March 25 new guidance on financial stability. Treasury's Bessent said:

Today’s proposed guidance would return the Council to prioritizing an activities-based approach where we focus first on risks that arise from specific activities and practices across markets, rather than single out individual firms.

Well, the prior CFO of Fannie Mae has long argued on his blog that Fannie and Freddie ERCF requirements essentially single them out and are not aligned with their fundamental economics and risk:

As soon as officials from the Trump administration began to discuss releasing Fannie and Freddie, interests that benefit competitively and financially from keeping them constrained in conservatorship and grossly overcapitalized concluded that the best argument they had against this move was that it would cause mortgage rates to rise, perhaps sharply, unless what has been termed the “implicit guaranty” on their mortgage-backed securities, or MBS, was replaced with an explicit government guaranty (which no one believes will happen).

I think these new guidelines open the door for those types of conversations. In other words, the activities-based approach undermines the argument for conservatorship. Now, there is a cost-benefit analysis that is required to designate any firm as systemically important. Now, in order to be designated, there is an off-ramp provision with the new FSOC framework. This is what GSE reform needs. In a perfect world I'd see an off-ramp to get out of this indefinite conservatorship as well as a rework of the ERCF to reflect actual risk.

Litigation Update

The Berkeley plaintiffs are pushing on the possibility of getting an upgraded damages model into the class action litigation. You can see the PDF legal filing yourself: 25-5113-2160327.pdf. The main event is the push for Reliance Damages:

My view is that these will be settled as part of the admin recap and release, and I think Fannie preferred, Freddie common, and preferred are in this lawsuit --- which was odd to leave out Fannie Mae commons.

Rop v. FHFA

Shareholders have now lost this 17-cv-00497-0121.pdf:

This loss effectively closes the remaining Collins remand cases. The Supreme Court had left open a narrow hypothetical of the termination of an FHFA director due to the removal restriction issue, but it is open no more.

Kelly v. United States

Oral arguments were on March 5, 2026. You can listen to them on the Federal Circuit's website here. The bottom line is that this largely revolves around tolling under the Tucker Act. In the past I would read into everything and try to understand the arguments on these legal issues, but I've learned from experience that the judge is going to decide for whatever outcome the judge wants, and I'm not sure that words really mean anything except hoops that the legal opinions then have to jump through to assert their dominance. When you sue the government, it feels like judges tend to rule in favor of the government most of the time, so I guess that's what I've just come to expect, and this one is no different, but I wish the shareholders well.

Prior CEO of Freddie Mac Donald Layton

On March 24, prior CEO of Freddie Mac Donald Layton put together a Furman Center article entitled "President Trump Paves the Way for the FHFA to Reform and Reduce GSE Capital Requirements" in response to Trump's March 13th executive orders on housing (above). This article marks a shift in his arguments, now for the first time, he supports the direction of presidential administrative action by arguing that the reform of capital requirements is basically something they can easily execute and should do. Doing this is only relevant in executing the path to exit conservatorship. Inside of a never-ending conservatorship, capital requirements would never matter. They only matter for getting out.

Pro Forma Restructuring

I expect the government to convert its SPSPA stock to common and exercise the warrants. Rough math: if Fannie and Freddie make $30B per year and have $190B of SPSPA outstanding and trade at a 12x valuation and the government takes 80% warrants and the SPSPA is converted at the current market price, we can calculate dilution and estimate the future price of commons.

$190B / $6.75 market price is 28B more shares on top of the 9B shares they'd get from the warrants. If you're looking at 37B shares outstanding and the companies trade at 12x earnings, you're looking at $9.72 a share in common. That's napkin math.

The junior preferred like FNMAS to get reinstated as senior equity and would be valued based on their conversion to common at the IPO price or the reinstatement of dividends. FNMAS, with its premium-to-market dividend terms, would trade over $25 with dividend reinstatement.

Risks

The main risk is the timeline. This has been ongoing since 2008 and even since they started retaining earnings in 2019. The lawsuits haven't helped end this. We really need administrative action to get this done, in my opinion, if it's going to get done soon. The alternative is wildly overcapitalized, never-ending conservatorships where we kind of live in an upside down world of SPSPA just taking everything off the top for the US Treasury eventually.

The second main risk is restructuring terms. No one actually knows what they are yet. They haven't been announced. Restructurings are not easy, but there are professional companies that work through them that give guidance on these things.

Anything could happen to derail all of this success and what is essentially a deal to be signed by Trump, ready to go, that makes housing in America more affordable. I don't think it will, but crazier things have happened here in Fanniegate land.

Summary and Conclusion

I have been in this trade since mid-2014. I have been surprised to see the price of Fannie and Freddie preferred come down so much this year, but that's how things go, I guess. Overall, I think this has been a larger than life learning experience for me on undervaluing volatility and overestimating timeline confidence.

I do not see significant security in Fannie and Freddie common shares and don't really understand the investment thesis there and see it as more of a confused gamble, but I have come to respect the concept of reflexivity by George Soros and how that sort of applies here in my opinion: when the price goes up, dilution goes down, if you will, and that retroactive backfill of security is not something really discussed much in Graham's Security Analysis.

I do see significant security in Fannie and Freddie preferred shares, recommend them, and own them. They're all I own, essentially. I believe we get admin action in the next few months, if not sooner.

data.title: An Interesting Multi-Faceted Utility: Artesian Resources
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

As analysts, covering Dividend Champions is something we especially enjoy doing. These are publicly traded companies with at least 25 consecutive years of payout raises.

Why is this the case? While the broader market fixates on the volatility of quarterly earnings beats or speculative growth, we appreciate the discipline that it requires to maintain a multi-decade streak of rising dividends.

A 25-plus-year reputation of dividend growth implies that a company has navigated at least two full economic cycles, a variety of geopolitical events, and so forth.

This brings us to our focus for today, which is Artesian Resources (ARTNA). When Kody last covered it with a Buy rating in December, he was encouraged by its double-beat in Q3. The company's continued allocation to infrastructure upgrades and new buildouts was another positive. What's more, ARTNA's interest coverage ratio was vigorous. Sealing the deal, shares were moderately undervalued at the time. In our January listicle, we liked ARTNA for the same reasons.

Fast forward to today, and we're reaffirming our Buy rating. The water utility invested a record amount in 2025 in water and wastewater infrastructure. We think that ARTNA will receive the bulk of what it requested in its ongoing rate case with Delaware's Public Service Commission, or PSC (DEPSC), too. The company's interest coverage ratio remains admirable for its industry. Finally, shares are a better value now than they have been in recent months.

The Ongoing Rate Case And Infrastructure Investments Are Growth Tailwinds

On March 12th, ARTNA released its earnings report for the fourth quarter ended December 31st, 2025. The utility's total operating revenue increased by 4.3% over the year-ago period to $28.02 million in the quarter.

What elements contributed to this mid-single-digit percentage topline growth during the fourth quarter? The primary catalyst for ARTNA's total operating revenue growth was the implementation of two temporary rate increases in Delaware (water and wastewater). These were allowed while the utility waited for a decision on its 10.75% net incremental increase in revenue on its rate case filed last April with Delaware's Public Service Commission. This helped total water sales revenue edge 2.9% higher year-over-year to $22.30 million for the quarter.

The other utility operating revenue (wastewater) and non-utility operating revenue (Service Line Protection Plans) categories also had a positive impact in the fourth quarter. The former posted $3.82 million in total revenue during the quarter, which was up 10.2% over the year-ago period. That was made possible by the expansion of its wastewater customer bases in Sussex County, Delaware, and Cecil County, Maryland. The latter's total operating revenue rose by 10% year-over-year to $1.89 million for the quarter. This was powered by a mix of a price increase on its Service Line Protection Plan and growth in its residential customers enrolling in such plans.

ARTNA's diluted EPS climbed 8.1% over the year-ago period to $0.40 in the fourth quarter. A 40-plus basis point expansion in the net profit margin to 14.8% is how diluted EPS growth outpaced total operating revenue growth during the quarter. Temporary rate increases and growth in the higher-margin wastewater and Service Line Protection Plan businesses were responsible for this underlying margin expansion for the quarter.

Of the 10.75% net increase in annual revenue (a $9.4 million increase) that ARTNA is seeking from DEPSC, we think most of this will be approved. Historically, regulators in the state have approved 65% to 75% of the utility's initial request.

Aside from the history of past rate cases, the key advantage that ARTNA has is that much of its costs are tied to mandatory PFAS and aging infrastructure upgrades. This is important to note because Delaware's Senate Bill 59, which took effect on January 1st, 2026, requires utilities to prove that all requested costs are prudent. It doesn't get any more prudent or necessary than complying with mandatory environmental regulations.

ARTNA is likely to concede some of its administrative costs in exchange for DEPSC approving the PFAS and wastewater infrastructure. We anticipate that the rate case will be decided within the next few months.

We think the projections from the sole analyst in FAST Graphs covering ARTNA are reasonable. In 2026, the analyst estimate is for a 9.5% decline in diluted EPS to $2.

What's holding back ARTNA this year is the carrying costs of investments made in its new assets to be proactive with PFAS regulations. Depreciation and higher operating expenses without the full benefit of the permanent rate increase are going to temporarily pressure profitability.

But as ARTNA has a full year of earning a regulated return on its record $58.8 million in investments in 2025, diluted EPS is expected to rebound 10% to $2.20 in 2027.

Financially, the water utility is also doing well. The company's interest coverage ratio was 3.6x in 2025. That's a high degree of financial stability for a regulated utility (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to ARTNA's Q4 2025 Earnings Press Release).

Fair Value Is Above $40 A Share

In the last two months, ARTNA's shares have returned -5%. That was almost in line with the -4% total return posted by the S&P 500 index (SP500) over that time.

From our perspective, this has further cemented its status as a compelling value. The regulated utility is now trading at a forward 12-month PE ratio of 15.5, which is well below the 10-year average PE ratio of 22.6.

Moving forward, we believe that a fair value PE ratio of 20 is realistic. This represents a modest upward revision from our prior fair value multiple of 18.5.

What supports our fair value PE ratio of 20? Beyond a bit of noise in 2026 stemming from a likely reset period with the upcoming rate case decision, the company's growth profile is holding up.

After a potential 9.5% decrease in diluted EPS in 2026, diluted EPS is anticipated to rebound 10% to $2.20 by 2027. Beyond that time, the FactSet long-term annual growth consensus is 5.1% (which could even be a bit conservative given ARTNA's capex cycle). That would be comparable to the 10-year average CAGR of 5.8%.

The constraining factor to the valuation fully reverting to the 10-year average remains an elevated interest rate environment. Currently, the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) of 4.2% is much higher than the 10-year average yield of 2.7%, per Macrotrends. In the years ahead, we believe that the 10-year yield will remain in the high-3% to low-4% range.

Over the past decade, ARTNA's earnings yield has generally traded at around a 150 to 175 basis point spread to the 10-year yield. But with the company in a meaningful investment phase, this could warrant a compression in the spread to 75 to 100 basis points. That's what yields our fair value multiple of 20.

The calendar year 2026 is 21% behind us. This leaves another 79% of 2026 and 21% of 2027 still to come over the next 12 months. That produces a forward 12-month diluted EPS input of $2.04.

Using our fair value PE ratio of 20, we get a fair value of $41 a share. Against the current $32 share price (as of March 17th, 2026), this equates to a 22% discount to fair value.

If ARTNA reverts to fair value and meets growth forecasts, it could deliver a 33% total return (32% annualized) through March 2027. By the end of 2031, the water utility could generate 12% annual total returns.

Expect A Continued Trickle Of Dividend Growth

ARTNA's 4% forward dividend yield registers higher than the utility sector's 3.2% median forward yield. This is a roughly 40-basis-point widening of the spread since December. While Seeking Alpha's Quant System currently doesn't have a grade assigned to ARTNA for forward dividend yield or overall dividend yield (there are no grades for metrics), I would suspect that they are respectively A- and B+ (up from B+ and B grades in December).

ARTNA's income is also especially safe. To this point, the diluted EPS payout ratio is positioned to be in the low-60% range in 2026. That would be comfortably better than the 75% payout ratio that rating agencies like to see from the industry, per the Zen Research Terminal.

This is why the Zen Research Terminal pegs the risk of a payout reduction within the next 12 months at only 0.3%. In the next average recession, this risk remains low at 1%. That's why I would assume that ARTNA would maintain a B- grade from the Quant System for overall dividend safety.

This positions the regulated utility to extend its 29-year dividend growth streak. Put into perspective, that's much more established than the sector median of 8.8 years. This would likely warrant the Quant System issuing an A+ grade for overall dividend consistency (which would be unchanged from December).

Looking ahead, we think that ARTNA can sustain 4%+ annual dividend growth for the foreseeable future. That would leave it with the flexibility to sustainably fund its capex and drive continued growth. This is why we believe that ARTNA would likely still earn a C+ grade from the Quant System for overall dividend growth (the same as it was in December).

Risks To Consider

ARTNA is arguably primed to hang onto its status as a Dividend Champion. Still, the regulated utility has risks that could alter its investment thesis.

The most noteworthy risk to ARTNA remains its geographic concentration. Just as Kody noted in December, 97.3% of its 101,840 water utility customers (99,100) were located in Delaware (page 53 of 91 of ARTNA's 10-K Filing). All of its wastewater customers were also located in the state.

That magnifies the importance of the pending rate case with the DEPSC. If it doesn't get most of what it's requesting from state regulators to supplement its rising expenses, this would weigh on its profit margins.

While we're reasonably confident that ARTNA will receive the majority of what it's requesting in this rate case, future rate cases are especially worth watching. That's because older Americans are growing increasingly concerned about the costs of their utility services.

An AARP survey of over 1,000 Americans age 50 and older, conducted last April, found that at least three out of four were worried about rising utility costs. Since this voting bloc is highly influential, it could pressure regulators in future rate cases to push back more on requested increases.

One more risk to ARTNA is that its status as a regulated utility makes it a valuable target for state-sponsored hackers and cybercriminals. If any attempts to breach the utility's IT networks are successful, it could result in litigation, regulatory fines, and damage to its brand reputation.

Conclusion

ARTNA is an intriguing, under-the-radar water utility. The company's continued investments in its infrastructure and pending rate case set it up for healthy growth in 2027 and beyond. ARTNA's interest coverage ratio remains exceptionally strong for a regulated utility as well.

Topping things off, shares are even more undervalued now than they were a couple of months ago. This is how a company with modest growth prospects relative to the broader market has a realistic chance at outperforming over the next few years. That's why we're standing by our Buy rating.

data.title: Comex Report: Ignore The Paper Price And Watch The Physical Metal
data.description:
The CME Comex is the exchange where futures are traded for gold, silver, and other commodities. The CME also allows futures buyers to turn their contracts into physical metal through delivery. You can find more detail on the CME here (e.g., vault types, major/minor months, delivery explanation, historical data, etc.).

The data below looks at contract delivery where the ownership of physical metal changes hands within CME vaults. It also shows data that details the movement of metal in and out of CME vaults. It is very possible that if there is a run on the dollar, and a flight into gold, this is the data that will show early warning signs.

Gold

Despite the fall in gold prices this month, this March saw the highest deliveries for a minor month since last May. Last May was right near the tariff uncertainty, where there were massive spreads in the price, creating an arbitrage opportunity. This had gold traveling from London to New York and pushing up vault volumes. Deliveries have been strong ever since.

The 14k contracts delivered would have also been considered quite strong when looking at any period prior to the arbitrage dislocation last year. Deliveries averaged closer to 5k prior to 2025. While the price did fall in March, some of which could be due to Turkey dumping a chunk of reserves, demand for physical metal remains quite robust.

Figure 1: Recent like-month delivery volume

Another interesting data point is the total notional delivered. The chart below shows the dollar amount of deliveries for gold specific to the month of March. While this March had delivery volumes almost 5k contracts lower than last March, from a dollar perspective it actually exceeded last March by a wide margin ($2.1B or 38%).

Figure 2: Notional Deliveries

Net new contracts (contracts that open and settle for immediate delivery) show another clue to the strength. Nearly 12k contracts were opened and delivered immediately. This made up 82% of total delivery volume. It trailed only last January and May. This means that while the paper price of metal has been under pressure all month, physical demand remained very strong.

Figure 3: Cumulative Net New Contracts

Perhaps the most important data point is the actual metal leaving Comex vaults. When metal is “delivered,” it does not mean it's leaving the Comex. It means that Registered ownership is moving from one party to another. When the metal leaves the vault, that is where things get interesting.

As shown, last year saw a major influx as the arbitrage trade took hold, but that metal has been flowing back out. In the latest month, Registered metal continued to see a move down. This metal did not flow into Eligible, which stayed relatively flat. Instead, the metal left the vault entirely. At the same time, Eligible also saw continuous and slow outflows.

This all fits the story above, a buyer throughout the month opening contracts, taking immediate delivery, and then removing it from the vaults. This is a slow, methodical buyer who is taking advantage of the price dip.

Figure 4: Inventory Data

Looking ahead to the April delivery period (a major month for gold), we see a contract that is hovering near the lower end of the average.

Figure 5: Open Interest Countdown

With the recent drop in inventory, the open interest relative to physical stocks is in trend with the recent months.

Figure 6: Open Interest Countdown Percent

Bottom line, March saw continued strength in delivery volume, a decent amount of physical metal left the vault entirely, and now the Comex has to deal with a major month which could see billions more delivered and leaving the vault. Once physical inventories get low enough, the ability for paper shorts to push the price down will be nearly gone.

Silver

While the gold market is showing a futures price that is getting higher above the spot rate (which caused metal to move from London to New York last year), the silver market is experiencing the opposite problem, backwardation. The spot price is above the futures price. This is actually a bigger warning sign because it highlights the pressure being put on the physical market.

Market Signal: Backwardation often signals a strong immediate demand or a supply shortage in the current spot market. The market is willing to pay a premium for the asset now, as the shortage is typically expected to resolve over the long term.

The backwardation ended on March 23rd, but it is dipping back down already. With the major price dislocations of silver between the East and West, it is unlikely that this type of backwardation will resolve anytime soon.

Figure 7: Spot vs Futures

Silver delivery drifted down again when compared to December and all the major months in 2025 except for July. Volume was well north of the average prior to the 2025 delivery surge.

Figure 8: Recent like-month delivery volume

Switching to notional values and focusing on the month of March produces the chart below. While most of this is price appreciation (over 7,000 more contracts were delivered in Mar 2025 vs Mar 2026), it still shows a very strong appetite for physical metal. Almost $4B of silver was delivered in this March, which is a massive volume.

Figure 9: Notional Deliveries

Similar to gold, net new contracts were a major driver, with the second-highest amount since last September.

Figure 10: Cumulative Net New Contracts

Silver-eligible inventories have absolutely plummeted. Over 70M ounces of metal have left the Eligible category in 2026 alone. At this pace, Eligible will be empty by Q4 of this year.

Figure 11: Inventory Data

Registered metal (metal available for delivery) is also seeing major outflows. Since the start of the year, 50M ounces have left the vault. That means 120M ounces, or $9B, of silver will have completely left the Comex in 2026. This has happened continuously and without pause day after day. If this trend continues at the current pace, Registered will be completely empty before September of this year.

Figure 12: Inventory Data

As we approach April delivery, the silver contract is at the lower end of the trend, but as we have seen, it will really come down to net new contracts.

Figure 13: Open Interest Countdown

On a relative basis, the open interest is actually on the higher side due to the massive drawdown in inventories.

Figure 14: Open Interest Countdown Percent

Conclusion

Every single data point above points to a singular outcome: physical metal is being bought and removed from Comex vaults on a consistent and continuous basis. This is a steady pull that is happening slowly day after day, week after week, and month after month. No panic buying, no massive moves. Just a drip, drip, drip of metal leaving the Comex. This is strategic buying with steady demand. The paper price is not having any impact. This is intentional accumulation of physical metal.

If trends continue, all the metal will be drained from Comex by the end of the year. Likely, something will happen before that occurs. They will refill coffers or start forcing cash settlement. It is getting harder to refill the coffers, though, as physical supplies remain tight around the world. If Comex starts forcing cash settlement, then it will be clear that the paper and physical markets have fully dislocated. So just remember, if you don’t hold it, you don’t own it!

Original Post
