data.title: Energy Is My Favorite Sector - I'm Getting Ready To Buy It On Weakness
data.description:
Introduction

I have written a lot on energy. That may not come as a surprise to many. Last year, I covered it so much that I was worried it would bother some people. Luckily, that wasn't the case.

One of my favorite articles I have written on energy is the one titled "Energy Is, By Far, My Favorite Sector For 2026." In that article, I had to admit that I had become too bullish too soon due to risks like the supply glut. However, I also made clear why I remained bullish, which included these three reasons.

  • Major producers started to reduce output, mostly due to unfavorable prices and dwindling Tier 1 reserves in places like the Permian Basin.
  • Demand growth was likely underestimated.
  • Investors were extremely bearish.

My strategy is always focused on buying high-quality companies in unloved areas. I buy them as long as they are out of favor. Often, that results in being somewhat early. However, that's a risk I am willing to take, as I believe that nobody can time the market.

So far, that has worked out quite well in the energy space, as the sector is indeed the best place in 2026. Year-to-date, energy is up 36% while I am writing this. That's 40 points above the return of the S&P 500 and almost 30 points above the 7% return of the materials sector.

Moreover, the surge was so wild that the ratio of energy stocks to the S&P 500 (see the chart below) has skyrocketed to November 2023 levels. In other words, energy has erased more than two years of severe underperformance within the span of less than four months.

With that said, this isn't a victory lap. Although I am super glad my thesis paid off so well this year, allowing me to beat the market, I'm not done. My thesis wasn't that energy would do well for a few months. My thesis was, and still is, that energy is the place to be for years to come.

That's what this article is about.

So, let's get right to it!

Energy Crashed The Market. Ignore That Part

In January and February, energy also led the market. Back then, it was much easier for me to take credit, as the surge was based on the factors that I actually predicted. This included accelerating economic growth (i.e., ISM Manufacturing Index), short-covering of bearish energy investors, and the absence of new bearish supply headlines.

Then the Iran War happened. That's what put energy on everyone's radar, as oil prices suddenly started to dominate global headlines. This includes headlines like Australian gas stations are literally running out of gasoline and other refined fuels. From a supply chain point of view, that makes sense, as the Strait of Hormuz handles roughly a quarter of seaborne global oil supply and a fifth of liquid natural gas. Once that supply gets cut, it's basically a ripple effect through global supply chains, as prices spike and eventually even physical supplies start to dwindle. That's a major economic risk and the reason why the market has become so nervous.

Although I have said for many years that volatile geopolitics are a reason why I own defense stocks, energy, and stock exchanges that benefit from volatility, I did not predict the Iran War.

And, while my thesis looks good, I have also often said that I do not want to see sky-high energy prices. As bullish as I may be, I do not want an environment of >$90 WTI. That's based on the simple reason that economies need affordable energy. Because many oil companies are efficient, all energy bulls need is $75-$85 WTI to enjoy high cash flows without elevated economic risks that could break the consumer.

I am painting with a broad brush, but that's basically the gist of it.

Right now, I'm bringing all of this up because, despite my bullish view, my thesis isn't that you should chase momentum here. Although nobody seems to know how long this conflict could last, I know that I do not want to add to energy sector stocks in an environment where it's on everyone's mind. As most of us likely bought last year, we don't have to chase here.

No, I am writing this article to tell you that we need to closely watch energy to buy it from the people who may sell it to us once the Iran War headlines start to indicate less supply risk.

At that point, I believe the market will start to treat energy again as the "dead money" trade it was last year, likely failing to realize that major bullish shifts are happening under the surface.

Suddenly, Energy Supply Is Bullish

While I am writing this, NYMEX WTI trades in the low-$90s. By the time you are reading this, it could be $80, $120, anything in between, or something even "wilder." That's how fast headlines move.

I'm fine with that, as I don't care about short-term prices. My point is that at current prices, something very interesting is happening. As you may have expected, money managers are very bullish. CME Group (CME), which owns NYMEX, shows that money manager net long positions are at one of the highest levels in years (the green line in the chart below).

Producers, however, have started to go big into bearish investments. That's not because they are bearish but because whenever oil spikes, they tend to hedge themselves.

Especially after two tough pricing years, they can finally de-risk themselves.

That's an important fact, yet not the development that matters. What matters even more to my thesis is that oil producers are not increasing production. Technically, an environment of elevated prices, geopolitical tailwinds, and more hedges is a great opportunity to boost supply a bit.

However, according to the recently released Dallas Fed Energy Survey, that's not happening. Looking at the oil production indicator, we see that there's no change at all, as the number of companies that cut production equals the number of companies boosting output.

That's due to an increasing difference between small and large players. Roughly 70% of large E&P (exploration and production) companies reported no changes in drilling plans. If we include the fact that 80% of U.S. production is dominated by these companies, it explains why we shouldn't expect higher output.

Between 2013 (start of the shale revolution) and 2021, these were mostly focused on boosting output. In recent years, they shifted their focus to reserve protection and free cash flow growth.

This is important to keep in mind, and accelerated by two other factors. One factor is that companies have found out that the consistent rush for higher output resulted in too much supply. That hurt prices. Basically, for a full decade, energy companies spent aggressively on CapEx without creating shareholder value. That's changing now. The other reason is resource depletion. While my opinion is that the U.S. will not run out of good spots to drill, companies don't have the favorable Tier 1 resources they had a few years ago.

To give you a great example, American companies are still ultra-efficient. Existing wells are breakeven in the $40s WTI range. This means that most are printing cash right now.

However, as most production in the U.S. relies on fracking, these companies deal with high decline rates, which means new wells quickly lose momentum, which forces producers to drill new wells to maintain production.

Last year, the average price needed to drill a new well was $65 in the Permian. Now, it's $67. In other words, prices below $67 will likely result in declining output over time. Also, we clearly see the impact of inflation on rising breakevens. That's bullish for oil prices, as oil companies are basically being forced to be more prudent.

I also thought about the bearish arguments.

  • When the war in Iran ends, the risk premium will fade. That's true, without a doubt. And I honestly hope it happens. After all, that's when I will buy more energy. What the market often forgets is that the physical market isn't the market we trade every day. Even if oil prices were to slump, we're now dealing with an environment where the biggest growth engine of the past 20 years (U.S. shale) is running out of momentum. Especially when oil prices drop, that factor will become stronger.
  • Even better, after the war, all excess OPEC supply will be gone. There's no glut anymore, and prices won't be high enough to get any major global player to boost output again. In fact, I expect an Iranian de-escalation to result in a continuation of my growth acceleration thesis. That would even increase oil demand.
  • I would even make the case that supply has become so tight that a 1-2 million barrel a day (depending on who you ask) demand decline due to temporary growth headwinds can be absorbed.

That's why I am so bullish on energy and believe we should closely watch it for new opportunities the moment favorable ceasefire headlines cause oil and energy to drop.

Where I'm looking For Value

I like two (geographic) areas to invest in.

  • The American Permian Basin.
  • The Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin.

The first basin is America's biggest oil basin. While this basin is also losing steam due to dwindling Tier 1 reserves, it's the number one place where American drillers expect growth to come from, as the chart below shows.

In this area, I like high-margin producers like Diamondback Energy (FANG), Permian Resources (PR), Devon Energy (DVN), and landowners like Texas Pacific Land (TPL) and LandBridge (LB). Moreover, I like companies like WaterBridge (WBI) and Western Midstream (WES), which focus on bottlenecks like produced water and pipelines (egress).

There are obviously more picks, but these are some of my favorites for the years ahead.

Meanwhile, Canada has the benefit that its oil sands production has no decline rates. These are massive areas where oil is often produced in surface mines. These operations come with huge upfront costs, yet once they are operational, they allow producers to enjoy the benefits for decades with low breakeven prices. And while Canada has major takeaway problems like limited pipeline capacity, I believe these risks will fade over time, as the importance of Canada's supply will likely get a lot more emphasis.

In this area, I like the big guys, including Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), which is a pure-play producer, and its peers, Suncor (SU) and Cenovus (CVE), which both have refining assets as well.

Right now, I'm watching all of these, prepared to buy on weakness, as I have little doubt they all have what it takes to generate substantial alpha for years to come.

Takeaway

My takeaway today is simple. Energy got crushed last year. This year, energy is "crushing it."

While my thesis looks good, that's not the point, as I'm a long-term investor. The Iran War may have accelerated the thesis, yet it has nothing to do with the core of why I'm bullish.

What matters to me is dwindling reserves, more proof of prudent capital allocation, a focus on shareholder returns, and fertile growth for economic growth broadening. Under these circumstances, I see long-term alpha in energy.

And given current headlines, I'm waiting for a bigger pullback in energy. The moment that happens, I happily buy the shares of people who think energy will go back to underperforming the market.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk is a prolonged period of oil prices below $67/$65. That would hurt American oil producers, make new wells unprofitable, and potentially lead to lower output. While it's long-term bullish due to subdued supply growth, it's the factor that made energy such a tough place to be last year.

On top of that, because energy is cyclical, an increase in recession odds could hurt demand over time.

And, in general, most energy stocks are very volatile. Please do your own due diligence before putting any capital to work in this sector.

data.title: Gambling.com: More Risks Are Surfacing (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis review

Gambling.com (GAMB) is one of the most underperforming names I have written on. Since my last article, where I rated it a strong buy, the stock has lost 64% of its value, and now it has an enterprise value of $240 million. This enterprise value is inclusive of the $160 million value of Odds Holdings, their largest acquisition. Meaning the rest of the business is roughly valued at $80 million.

As the title of my previous article entails, my main thesis was that, now that GAMB has transitioned to being a data provider instead of a marketing affiliate firm, the company has removed the headwind overhang associated with the secular decline of its legacy business due to Google SEO updates and AI disruption. The market has clearly priced in this secular decline, now that the acquired company is worth more than the legacy business. Even on the latest earnings call, management is more optimistic about growth expansion being realized in the subscription segment rather than in the marketing business.

Despite the drastic transition the firm is undertaking, the stock remains unattractive as momentum continues to be depressed. The Quant system has rated GAMB's momentum a D- given that YoY the stock has fallen by 71% and YTD it has fallen by 26.9%, the overall quant rating is a strong sell. Wall Street and Seeking Alpha analysts have maintained their strong buy ratings, stating that the market is still viewing the stock with the lens of its secularly declining business. New risks have surfaced for GAMB, making me wary of my initial rating; namely, the Swish lawsuit, the huge surge in debt and interest expenses, and the headwinds faced by sports betting as prediction markets become more pervasive. With the stated risks, I'm downgrading GAMB to a hold.

Legacy business is stagnating

The segment revenue charts below are illustrative of the business transition GAMB is undertaking. Performance marketing is in the last phase of the S-curve, while subscription revenue has recently spiked due to the inorganic growth from acquisitions. The advertising segment is growing decently; however, it remains the least contributor to overall revenue. When looking at total revenue, it's growing by double digits, masking the lack of organic growth.

In GAMB's case, organic growth mainly comes from casino revenue, as these are mostly the sites that pay for bettor leads. Compared to 2024, casino revenue grew 4.72%, from $92.2 million to $96.5 million, while sports revenue grew 172%, from $33.9 million to $65.09 million. Again, most of the growth from the sports segment was inorganic; in fact, organic revenue growth was -2% in 2025 vs. 2% in 2024 and 41% in 2023.

Now this means any hope of future topline growth in GAMB should be expected to come from the subscription business. We are not certain about the growth outlook of this segment, as the firm doesn't break down topline and EBITDA growth rates by individual business segments, e.g., OddsJam growth. One way to tell that OddsJam's growth is looking good is via the amendments to the acquisition deal. Just a quick recap on the original deal: GAMBS was expected to pay an additional $80 million as earnouts, contingent on Odds Holdings meeting performance targets, one of the targets being that the firm is expected to double 2024 EBITDA in 2026. The recent amendment is a tell that Odds Holdings is heading to hit those targets now that earnouts have converted to fixed payouts, with a balance estimated to be fully paid in April 2027, bringing the total acquisition value to $160 million when we exclude annual discounts.

Swish Lawsuit

My main issue with Odds Holdings is in regard to the ongoing lawsuit that was filed by Swish Analytics against the firm. The lawsuit claims that OddsJam scrapes modeled odds data from Swish's third-party clients like FanDuel and then resells it as its own. We mostly thought this case would be dismissed given that the data is public. Unfortunately, the case is still ongoing and could result in $100 million in damages to be paid if the case is lost. I find this risk to be material and should not be overlooked. Also, given that the case might be ongoing for longer than expected, we should expect legal expenses to weigh on margins going forward.

Financial Analysis

Q4 2025 Income Statement

In the latest quarter of 2025, GAMB increased revenue by 30% YoY to $46.24 million, which led to an estimate beating by $178,890.00. The growth was led by subscription revenue growing 440% to $11.82 million, followed by advertising growing 23% to $5.82 million and performance marketing growing 1% to $28.60 million. Cost of revenue increased to $6.89 million, resulting in gross profit increasing 19% to $39.34 million and landing margins at 85% vs. the sector margin of 53.93%. Operating expenses increased 117% to $64.87 million, caused by the restructuring expense of $32.50 million, mainly due to the earnout payment made to Odds Holdings shareholders as per the newly amended deal dictated. This led the firm to realize an operating loss of $25.53 million and a GAAP EPS of -$0.77, missing estimates by -$0.93. For Q1 of 2026, analysts estimate revenues of $40.25 million, implying a YoY decline of 1% and a QoQ decline of 13%. I find these estimates to be reasonable and in line with the firm's seasonal growth levels and the persistent decline of SEO-driven revenue.

FY2025 Income Statement

Full-year revenue also grew 30% to $165.45 million, with subscription revenue growing 382% to $41.13 million, advertising growing 17% to $20.75 million, and performance marketing growing 2% to $103.57 million. Restructuring costs amounted to $62.01 million, leading to operating expenses growing 117% to $181.98 million and operating losses growing 189% to $31.79 million. For the full year of 2026, management is guiding for revenue ranging between $170 million and $180 million, implying 8.79% YoY growth on the high-end. I find this guidance aggressive, implying that SEO declines are temporal, which, in my view, they actually look secular. During the earnings call, management hinted at a product release that will be revenue beneficial to the marketing business, which would explain the aggressive guide. I wouldn't want to take this at face value, especially now that the previous CEO has departed.

FY2025 Balance Sheet

Adding to the concerns regarding the stagnating growth of the legacy business is the highly leveraged GAMB's balance sheet. Cash increased 15% to $15.81 million, while current debt increased 199% to $10 million and long-term debt increased 458% to $108.62 million. If we don't see significant topline growth in the subsequent years, interest expenses will weigh heavily on earnings, leading to low free cash flow and a cash balance falling lower than current debt. We can see that most of the assets' value is trapped under intangibles; some of these are recently acquired assets that are not estimated to bring any medium-term cash flows; therefore, near-term debt coverage risks remain an overhang that can only be alleviated by free cash flow growth or stock issuance.

While performance marketing is stagnating and still accounting for 62% of overall revenue, we are also seeing interest expenses increasing, not good. Yes, earnings were negative in the last quarter due to restructuring costs; I wouldn't want to see them persisting to be negative for operational reasons. But this is where things are heading if this trend continues. The lowest level of interest expenses was seen in 2022 at $1.3 million, and has since increased 550% to $8.45 million in 2025.

Even though Odds Holdings' acquisition is pivotal for GAMB, it has surfaced many risks to the stocks that shouldn't be ignored. When I read a lot of articles from my fellow SA analysts, the common theme is that, as the stock continues to fall, management will opportunistically take advantage of this undervaluation to purchase more shares. In 2025 the firm has retired 672,000.00 worth of shares to the value of $5.6 million. The chart below clearly illustrates that the company has been purchasing fewer shares over the quarters, and the stock continues to tank. Now that the cash position is even more pressured by the debt levels, I don't see any aggressive buybacks in the coming quarters, meaning the stock price will remain at these levels if we don't see new buyers.

Bull Case

Even though I've switched bearish, GAMB remains a highly profitable firm trading at a discounted valuation. In fact, the firm exceeds all its industry peers on EBIT margins (21.37% vs. a sector margin of 11.10%) and most peers on FCF margins (16.81% vs 10%). And looking at the FCF chart below, we can see that free cash flow exceeded the $40 million mark on a trailing basis in the second half of 2025, the decline to $18.24 million in the last quarter was due to the Dec 2025 earnout payment to Odds Holdings shareholders. Management expects not just topline growth but also margin expansion from the data service business, if these expectations come to fruition, not only will GAMB be able to service its interest expenses, but it will also lower its debt burden and continue purchasing shares, more aggressively. I'll be paying more attention to FCF margin growth in subsequent quarters, as this is one indicator that could switch me to a bull again.

Conclusion

GAMB remains to be undervalued; a PE multiple of 9 vs. a sector median of 14.50 is definitely a steal. But with the risks that have recently surfaced, I think the discounted valuation is deserved until the dust settles. What I'm mainly looking forward to is the growth potential of the subscription business. Outside of the Swish lawsuit, one headwind for this business is the pricing pressure that might come from the enterprise side. As these sportsbooks begin to lose market share to prediction markets due to lack of vig, this will push them to cut costs on data expenses to maintain high margins. For these reasons I'm downgrading GAMB to a hold.

data.title: Reddit Starting To Look Straight-Up Attractive At This Price
data.description:
I covered Reddit (RDDT) back in February, assigning a buy rating (upgraded from hold put forward in July). Since then, the stock has been down +15%. At first glance, you’d assume that the stock is heading in the opposite direction of what I’d want, but I’m not so sure. I felt RDDT was quite expensive last time around, arguing, “I assign RDDT a buy rating, but it’s closer to hold territory than nearly all of my other buy recommendations.”

Yet Reddit’s tough start to 2026 has improved the valuation quite a bit, and the upside here is quite substantial. Hence, my update article. The valuation has changed enough to improve my outlook overall. I still wouldn’t call RDDT cheap, per se, but in a world of high PE ratios, it’s not outside of the norm either, in my opinion. As such, I’m keeping my buy rating and putting RDDT firmly in the middle of my buy recommendations rather than at the trailing edge.

Reddit Remains One Of The More Human-Credible Websites Out There

Years back, I used to do a fair bit of SEO writing. As with many an industry, seeing how the sausage is made can dampen one’s appetite. While I typically went out of my way to ensure that I was writing accurate information and otherwise adding value to readers, keyword and content stuffing was for many the norm. Unfortunately, low-grade content leads to a low-grade World Wide Web. People complain about AI slop now, but really, the previous iteration of the Internet could be viewed as SEO slop.

Reddit, however, has by and large been an oasis in terms of genuine, authentic content. Make no mistake, you’ll find spammers and stuffers on Reddit, along with bots, AI comments, etc. By and large, however, the Reddit community is pretty good at sniffing such content out (although I’m sure some junk content does go undetected).

As AI gains prominence and AI-generated content proliferates, Reddit likely will still remain one of the better sources of human-generated content. The ability to niche down on the website is especially useful. There are plenty of specific topical sub-Reddits to explore, including things like hiking, taking care of dogs, and removing earwax (yes, there is actually a rather busy sub-Reddit that focuses on earwax removal.)

If Reddit can keep its website relatively bot- and AI-free (big if), the site will continue to be a major value to everyday people, AI developers (who often buy access to Reddit’s data), and advertisers, among others. Of course, there are no guarantees here, but Reddit is still one of the better bets in terms of human-generated content and engagement.

Reddit is also one of the better sources of fresh data for AI developers to tap into. I covered this a bit more in my prior article on Reddit, but essentially, AI developers are running out of sources of fresh data. The more data a developer has access to, the more they can train their models. The more they train their models, the more powerful they become. Now, with developers running out of fresh data, there’s a risk that it’ll slow progress.

Anyway, Reddit has begun to monetize selling data to AI developers, with the larger contracts worth many tens of millions. Keep in mind, the content itself is largely user-generated, which should help Reddit keep costs low and margins high.

RDDT Fundamentals And Valuation Considerations

RDDT still isn’t cheap, per se, with a TTM GAAP PE ratio of about ~48. The non-GAAP is more attractive at ~28, and the forward non-GAAP looks even better at ~19. The primary difference in these two numbers is Stock-Based Compensation, which is illustrated below. While SBC does dilute shareholder value, RDDT is still producing growth that you’d expect with a hot technology startup. These numbers mark a solid improvement from when I last looked at RDDT, when it had 33.12 non-GAAP PE TTM and 57.30 GAAP PE TTM ratios.

Reddit SBC

YOY revenue growth weighs in at just over 69%. This is expected to slow to 46.5% revenue growth going forward, but that’s still blistering growth. Meanwhile, the strong Gross Profit and Net Income Margins (TTM) reflect a mature, profitable company, and are attractive even by the standards of the tech industry and the typically high profitability it enjoys. Someday, growth will taper off, but that’s likely not going to happen for a good long while.

Reddit’s balance sheet is also healthy, with the company carrying only $310 million in total liabilities, versus ~2.477 billion in cash and short-term assets and ~$590 million in accounts receivable. So while Reddit is enjoying hot startup-like growth, it has a balance sheet that you’d expect from a mature, profitable company.

I actually initiated Reddit as a hold, primarily because I am worried about advertising-dependent websites right now. I still am wary of advertising-dependent companies, and that is something I’d keep an eye on. If growth were to drop to, say, 20-30% YOY, I’d likely start to get nervous.

My fellow Seeking Alpha analysts, in aggregate (which may include my prior buy recommendation from February), rate it a buy. Wall Street also comes away with a buy rating. The quant tools are a bit more skeptical, it seems, with a hold rating. Valuation gets a poor mark at D-, and I do agree this is still a pretty pricey stock, but I believe the growth and profitability justify the high valuation. If RDDT continues to slide, I’ll likely view that as an opportunity to add more on the cheap.

Why I Could Be Wrong

RDDT is still pretty pricey as stocks go, and I may simply be overestimating its potential. AI presents opportunities, but also threats, including the risk of the site being drowned out via AI-generated content and engagement. Reddit might also simply go out of style. It’s not uncommon to find people complaining about moderators, company policies, etc. Someday, negative sentiments may fester and spread, and the audience might move on.

Key Takeaway: Reddit Runs With The Pack

After a rather tepid buy recommendation in February, I’m upgrading RDDT in the middle of my to-buy pack. This is a solid investment given the high upside. The risks are considerable but not enough to scare away investing, at least not for me. This is a stock I'd keep an eye on, with growth being perhaps the most crucial barometer for now.

data.title: Big BDC Dividend Yields: 1 Golden Opportunity And 1 Value Trap
data.description:
The business development company (BIZD) sector has been selling off recently as private credit has come under increasing scrutiny and has suffered from numerous negative media headlines recently. As a result, numerous popular publicly traded BDCs now trade at large discounts to net asset value as well as their own historical averages, presenting potentially compelling buying opportunities for income-focused and long-term-oriented value investors who are willing to be patient during the current negativity.

However, not all BDCs are created equal, with some having legitimately high risk. I think that at least somewhat validates the discounts they are currently experiencing.

With that in mind, in this article, I'm going to compare two of these opportunities and share which one I think is worth buying right now and which one I'm steering clear of.

Key Risks Facing The BDC Sector

Before I get into the details of these two BDCs, the biggest risks facing BDCs right now:

  • The Fed has been on a path towards cutting interest rates, which tends to weigh on BDCs, since most of their investments are in floating-rate loans. Whenever the Fed cuts rates, their net investment income tends to decline. This has already led several BDCs to cut their dividends.

  • Additionally, many BDCs have fixed-rate debt maturing in the near term, which they are having to refinance at materially higher interest rates than they were underwritten at, as much of this debt was taken on about five years ago following COVID-19, when the Fed slashed rates aggressively. Today, while the Fed is in the process of cutting rates, long-term interest rates have risen significantly over the past half-decade, especially after oil prices skyrocketed following the war in Iran.

  • Moreover, there is growing concern about the health of the BDC sector, as capital has flooded into the space in recent years at a much faster clip than new loans have become available. This has created a supply-demand imbalance, which has led some to fear that underwriting standards have slipped, and has also seen spread compression leading to less attractive returns with potentially increased risks.

  • Additionally, the war in Iran and the disruption to oil prices are leading many to have concerns that we could be on the cusp of a material recession, which would likely lead to a spike in non-accruals and even defaults, which would mean lower income and lower NAV for most BDCs.

  • Last but not least, there is concern that portfolios that have considerable exposure to software loans could be disrupted by AI.

A Shrinking Ice Cube With Troubling Fundamentals

The first BDC I'm going to discuss is FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK). It is a large BDC with a roughly $13 billion portfolio diversified across 232 companies, and its portfolio is not that defensively positioned, with only about 57.8% of it allocated toward first-lien senior secured loans, well below the BDC peer average. Additionally, its underwriting performance has been fairly lackluster, with non-accruals at 5.5% at cost and 3.4% on a fair-value basis, which is among the highest in the BDC sector. Moreover, non-income-producing investments comprise a whopping 9.5% of fair value. Furthermore, software services make up about 16.4% of its portfolio, which is a sector that is considered riskier in the current AI disruption environment. As a result, FSK was recently downgraded by Moody's to junk from investment grade.

The balance sheet is in okay shape, with net debt to equity at 1.22 times, which is below its 1.25 times upper limit on its target leverage range, though it does not have much flexibility there at all. Meanwhile, FSK recently had to refinance $1 billion worth of fixed-rate loans that were at a 3.4% interest rate that matured in 2026, which means that its new debt will be pressuring its net investment income moving forward, including in Q1. Additionally, 90% of its liabilities mature in 2027, which keeps this near-term maturity risk somewhat limited, other than its January 2026 refinancing that it recently had to do. Its liquidity of $3.8 billion allows it some wiggle room to handle potential issues.

The Biggest Indictment Against FSK

Perhaps the biggest indictment against FSK, though, is its consistent and substantial NAV erosion. For example, over the past year alone, its NAV per share has declined from $22.75 per share, or 11.6% of its NAV in a single year, which effectively wipes out the dividend it paid out over that period of time. This past quarter alone saw a $0.89 per share decline from realized and unrealized losses. This paints a picture of a very unsustainable income machine that is a shrinking ice cube, even though its dividend yield is attractive.

This just showed up in the company's recent dividend cut from 70 cents per share in Q4 to 48 cents per share in Q1, which includes a 45-cent per share base dividend and a 3-cent per share supplemental dividend. Perhaps the most jarring was how analysts pressed management on the earnings call about the prospects of buybacks, with the price-to-NAV ratio showing a massive discount to book value. However, management was non-committal, which signals that its alignment with shareholders is questionable at best. This stands out in stark contrast to Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), and several others, which have bought back stock aggressively whenever it trades at a discount to NAV. Meanwhile, FSK's fee structure, with a 1.5% base management fee and a 17.5% incentive fee, is not particularly attractive relative to that of peers. Their refusal to consider cutting the fee following their poor performance stands out as a negative contrast to other BDCs like Oaktree Specialty Lending (OCSL) and Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD), which have slashed their fees after poor performance.

Thus, while the discount to NAV is incredible at 51% presently and the dividend yield looks very attractive as well at 17.7%, the very poor underlying quality of the management team and its underwriting track record, as well as their recent downgrade to junk credit rating, signal to me that this is a very low-quality BDC. Even if its current discount to NAV is significantly overstated, there are other much higher-quality BDCs trading at deep discounts where the alignment with shareholders is much stronger, and that is therefore where I would much rather place my bets in a sector that is already besieged with uncertainty and headwinds.

The Blue-Chip BDC Built To Weather the Storm

The other BDC I'm going to discuss today is Golub Capital BDC (GBDC), which I think is a much more worthwhile investment right now. Like FSK, GBDC has a fairly large portfolio, with $8.6 billion worth of assets spread across 420 portfolio companies, with an average position size of just 0.2%, which makes it much more diversified than FSK. Additionally, GBDC is much more defensively positioned, with 92% of the portfolio invested in first-lien loans, which is much higher than the BDC average of 80% and FSK's 57.8%. Furthermore, its non-accruals are well below the sector average, just 0.8% on a fair-value basis and 1.3% at cost. Additionally, its underlying portfolio is performing well, with 89% of it rated 4 or 5 on a scale of 1 to 5, indicating a fairly small watch list. While its software exposure is very large at 27%, the company has 20 years and over a thousand software deals' worth of experience, with only five defaults, which amount to just 0.25% of the total $145 billion committed to software investments.

Moreover, management signaled a prudent and cautious approach to the sector that should serve it well through these uncertain times. Additionally, the credit ratings clearly see a high-quality business with a strong balance sheet, as Moody's awards GBDC a Baa2 stable credit rating, which is among the highest in the sector and two notches ahead of FSK's. That said, its leverage ratio of 1.23 times puts it on par with FSK's and in a slight and very small wiggle room below its upper target limit; however, the company's underwriting strength more than makes up for this.

Why GBDC's Long-Term Track Record Seals the Deal

Additionally, GBDC's NAV per share has been much more stable than FSK's, with only a very slight sequential decline this past quarter. Additionally, since its 2010 IPO, GBDC has generated a 9.5% annualized IRR on its NAV, which has outperformed a lot of its BDC peers and high-yield debt (HYG) sectors while delivering equity-like annualized total returns over that period. In fact, GBDC has generated a net gain rate since inception of 0.06% annualized, versus a peer average loss rate of negative 0.86%, reflecting its very strong underwriting and recovery capabilities.

Additionally, its dividend of 33 cents per share is well covered by NII, as this past quarter delivered 38 cents per share of adjusted NII, giving it about a 15% coverage rate on the base dividend. GBDC also plans to pay out about 50% of adjusted NII above its 33-cent-per-share base dividend, which gives it further upside as it finds ways to raise rates, including through interest rate hikes, while also leaving some capital behind to help sustain NAV and engage in buybacks. In fact, GBDC has been repurchasing shares actively, including in Q4 at an 8% discount to NAV, and today, with the NAV per share even higher at over 15%, buybacks are likely still active and even more accretive, especially with their retained cash after the dividend. Insiders are also well aligned, with 8% insider ownership, and with a 10.5% dividend yield, even before considering supplementals, GBDC's dividend looks sustainable and attractive.

The Rare BDC I'm Buying While Others Run for the Exits

While GBDC is not nearly as cheap as FSK, the insider alignment, the underwriting quality, the credit rating, and the company's long-term track record are vastly superior. Therefore, I view GBDC as an attractive "Buy" on the recent dip in the BDC sector, while I am steering clear of FSK, even if it is ridiculously cheap. While GBDC is a small position in our portfolio, there are several other blue-chip, deeply undervalued BDCs with sustainable and attractive dividend yields, with strong insider alignment and stock buybacks. We are slowly but surely adding to our portfolio that yields about 8%, with below-market beta and long-term total return outperformance, at High Yield Investor.

data.title: AppLovin: 40% Growth Rate Is Sustainable
data.description:
By Nicholas Tan, Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments

In our previous analysis of AppLovin (APP), we expected its growth outlook to remain robust, driven by continued improvements to its AXON 2.0 ML engine. We further believe that the fast growth of the in-app advertising market would support its growth outlook, as we project its critical growth driver (app spending per year) would grow with a 3.9% CAGR. We expected the company to maintain its robust competitiveness in high fill rates and eCPM. We further believed that its App segment and platform synergies, such as cross-network advertising and software access to in-app user data, would support its growth outlook. Since our last coverage, the company has divested its Apps segment for $900 million on June 30th, 2025, and was aimed at streamlining its operations away from game development.

In this analysis, we cover the company again, as its stock price declined by 40.98% from its peak of $733.60 in December 2025 to $432.98 in March 2026, despite the strong 70% YoY growth in 2025 (excluding the App segment). To determine the potential reasons, we compile analysts' concerns regarding the company after it released its earnings.

Based on analysts' commentary, we believe that the potential reasons that could explain the decline in stock prices include decelerating Q1 2026 growth, higher competition from Meta, Google, and CloudX, and regulatory concerns. We thus analyze whether these reasons could justify the decline in stock price. We first analyze whether the company could sustain its competitiveness amid the higher competition from Meta (META), Google (GOOGL)(GOOG), and Cloud X by examining its market share and products. We then evaluate whether the regulatory breach allegations have any merit and could materially impact its growth outlook. We conclude by evaluating whether the company could continue to grow despite these competitive and regulatory concerns.

New Competitive Developments

We first evaluate whether the company could sustain its competitiveness amid the higher competitive developments by Meta, Google, and Cloud X. We thus compile the gaming ad network revenue share to evaluate their market share. We use the gaming market to analyze the company rather than other segments, such as social networking, because the company is an ad platform that primarily targets 3rd-party mobile games and does not have social platforms to sell ads, such as Meta. We use ad revenue share rather than in-app advertising market share, as ad revenue share measures the monetization rate for developers of different app platforms.

From 2024 to Q3 2025, the company’s market share improved from 25.2% to 28.0%. This highlights its leading position in the market and strong monetization rate compared to its competitors. Google AdMob’s market positioning declined from 2nd to 3rd, as its market share declined from 20.8% to 14.3%. Mintegral’s positioning then improved to 2nd, as Google AdMob declined, rising from 16.6% to 17.2%. In 2022, Meta was one of the top 10 ad platforms with the largest market share. However, in 2025, our data source did not list Meta in the top ad platform share rankings, which suggests that Meta’s share has declined from 2022 to 2025, and that its share is likely below Liftoff's (2.7%), as it is still a current player in the market.

To further analyze their market positioning, we compile each company’s competitive metrics. In terms of analyzing AI risks from Google and Meta, we were unable to compile specific quantitative data that could evaluate AI’s competitiveness due to a lack of data. In terms of competitive metrics, the metrics are defined below:

  • ECPM measures the revenue earned per 1,000 ad impressions.
  • Fill rate calculates how many ads were successfully filled.
  • Daily active users calculate the “number of unique users engaging with an app on a single day.

* Represents the average of available data.

We see that AppLovin has one of the highest eCPM and fill rates, showing its strong competitiveness in generating revenue and filling ad requests for its advertisers. Whereas Google AdMob and Meta Audience Network have lower eCPM and fill rate. We believe its strong performance metrics can explain its large market share, as the company is more competitive than its competitors. As highlighted in our previous analysis, its strong performance in these metrics can be due to the AXON 2.0 ('AXON') architecture, which uses AI and ML tools such as predictive analytics and reinforcement learning to improve targeted advertising for customers. As AXON collects more data over time, this improves its performance metrics over the long term, which enables advertisers to monetize their ads more effectively.

Meta had recently released its Meta Andromeda, its “AI-powered ad retrieval and ranking system,” which advances upon its previous ad ranking architecture and enables advertisers to target customers that are outside its original search filters if there is a high probability of purchase from customers. The new architecture utilizes AI and ML to predict these actions. “Before Andromeda, the system evaluated thousands of ads in each auction. After Andromeda, it can evaluate tens of millions of ads in fractions of a second,” highlighting the wider range of search compared to before. Meta’s Andromeda competes with the company’s AXON 2.0 architecture and could impact its future competitiveness. On the other hand, Google is also utilizing AI for its ad platform, such as smart-bidding, which uses ML to analyze “millions of signals in real time,” like user device, location, and search history, to adjust their bids for app advertising more effectively. This could weaken its competitiveness. Recently, start-up Cloud X introduced an AI-powered auction platform for app developers on its ad network, where app developers can sell their app advertising space using AI, and also provide developers with transparent data, such as auction results and performance, which the company does not provide.

MAX, the company’s mediation platform, collects device data for its AXON architecture, while its AppDiscovery, its user acquisition advertising platform, also collects data for its AXON architecture. These data sources include purchase history, search history, previous interactions with its ads, as well as “session information, and installation information of the mobile application.” Adjust, its analytics platform for advertisers, also tracks users' data within their app, such as ad revenue (user spending on their app), app installations, and reinstalls. These data are specific to customers’ interactions with their ads and can be used to train AXON’s performance, enabling AXON to suggest more targeted advertising for advertisers. Importantly, as AXON receives more training data from MAX, AppDiscovery, and Adjust, AXON’s performance improves over time. This highlights its key competitive advantage, whereby AXON can improve over time as it continuously gathers data and improves targeted advertising.

The company has a high number of ad requests per day (15 billion per day), which is much higher than Mintegral (3 billion per day), the 2nd largest player in the ad revenue market. This is despite Mintegral having 57% more daily active users than the company (1.4 billion). The company’s MAX mediation platform is also used by 73.2% of the top 200 grossing games (excluding 28 non-ad games and 1 unavailable game), whereas Google’s AdMob, 3rd player in the ad revenue market, only has 11.4%. We believe the high number of ad requests and high mediation usage among the top games show that the company has access to large amounts of user data, which can be used to further train its AXON architecture and sustain its competitive advantage.

To further analyze its competitive positioning, we rank the companies based on their competitive metrics.

The company ranks 2nd overall, with the 2nd-highest eCPM and the highest fill rate. However, its ranking for daily active users is 6th. Despite this, the company’s ranking aligns well with its largest market share. IronSource ranks 1st, due to its highest eCPM and fill rate ranking.

We determine overall that the company can sustain its competitive advantage. Its AXON model has access to large amounts of data through its high 72% share of the top 200 grossing gaming games and its high number of ad requests per day compared to Mintegral, which has the 2nd highest market share. We further expect AXON to improve over time as AXON gathers more data from user data from MAX, AppDiscovery, and Adjust to enable AXON to serve better targeted ads to customers, allowing the company to maintain its high fill rate and eCPM ranking. In its Q1 FY2025 earnings transcript, management also highlighted AXON’s continuous improvement over time, as they have “obviously seen an immense amount of growth. This thing continues to get better as it scales… As we get more impressions, more engagements with the ads, more conversions, the thing just continues to get smarter.” As such, we expect the company to maintain its high market share in the market. We further believe the competitive developments from Meta, Cloud X, and Google would have a limited impact on its competitiveness, as AXON’s improves over time with its large access to data from MAX, AppDiscovery, and Adjust.

Regulatory Breach Allegations

After determining that the company is unlikely to face competitive pressures from Cloud X, Meta, and Google. We then examine and compile the regulatory allegations by the SEC and short-sellers to determine whether the regulatory breach allegations have any merit and could materially impact its revenue growth.

The company is currently undergoing SEC probes on whether the company has undertaken “device fingerprinting” on its partners’ platforms, which goes against the service agreements of its partners. Device fingerprinting refers to collecting data such as IP addresses, user time zones, and client devices. The SEC is also investigating whether the company had collected user data from multiple platforms, assembling the data and creating a more accurate digital profile. These regulatory investigations were previously alleged by short-sellers such as Muddy Waters Research and Fuzzy Panda Research, whereby they indicated the company had undertaken these unpermitted data collection processes.

Muddy Waters Research claims that when users open an app integrated with AppLovin’s SDK ('MAX'), the company would generate persistent identifiers for the user, which link to the user's device. After generating this, Muddy claimed MAX would obtain device information regarding users, such as IP addresses and operating systems, to create a digital profile of the user so that the company can track users across different platforms. Muddy then alleged that they linked the user’s digital profile with the persistent identifiers, allowing the company to track users across multiple platforms. This would constitute a breach of the SEC’s regulation that companies are not allowed to trace users across multiple platforms.

In Muddy’s test, Muddy first opens Zynga with AppLovin’s SDK, which generates 2 tokens (persistent identifiers) on Zynga. Muddy would then collect the value of one of the tokens. In Zynga, Muddy then clicks on a Shopify (SHOP) ad, which would then pop up a mini web browser to connect the game with Shopify. They alleged that to open the Shopify ad, AppLovin inserted the token value into the mini web browser, which enabled AppLovin to track the user from Zynga to the mini web browser to Shopify. This would breach the SEC’s regulation and AppLovin’s partners’ platform service agreement, such as Apple, which does not allow fingerprinting and tracking user ID across multiple platforms. By tracking user IDs across platforms, AppLovin can determine whether a user has previously “already visited an advertiser’s website, added items to a cart, or started a checkout.” This enables AppLovin to retarget users who have a high chance of completing their purchase if users have previously not completed their purchase of products that were in the shopping cart. Thus, even if AppLovin initially loses the ad bid against Google and Meta, AppLovin can retarget these high-probability-of-purchase customers again by tracking user IDs across platforms. We believe this enables them to bid higher than their competitors for customers with high probability, and lower for customers with low probability of purchase, allowing AppLovin to effectively allocate ad spend for customers with high probability of purchase. As such, by tracking user IDs and retargeting these customers, this improves their AppDiscovery competitiveness as they have access to data that other competitors do not have.

Fuzzy Panda Research also used a similar method, whereby Fuzzy first searched Ridge wallets on Ridge’s website with AppLovin SDK integration. Fuzzy then played an AppLovin game, which promptly served Ridge ads, suggesting that AppLovin is tracking users from their partner's website to their game. In response to these allegations, the company has also published findings regarding user ID to disprove that they are tracking users, with their CEO indicating, "in apps, we comply with ATT on iOS. Our SDK collects only basic device information from public APIs the OS provides and allows, which is consistent with every major SDK."

We find that Muddy Water Research and Fuzzy Panda Research did not mention that their data collection methods and results were verified with a fully independent research institution, such as the Media Rating Council, which audits digital advertising data and verifies platform data by companies such as LinkedIn and Google. Without independent verification, we thus believe this reduces the credibility of their data collection process. The 2 short sellers have also collected the data themselves, which further reduces the independence of data collection. This is because their results could have been fabricated, which would mislead investors. Muddy Water Research and Fuzzy Panda Research are also short the company, which increases the conflict of interest, as both these short sellers would benefit from misleading investors. Previously, Fuzzy Panda Research’s short report on Napco was found to be inaccurate, whereby a global law firm employed by Napco to conduct an independent investigation “found no evidence to support the short seller’s allegations.” Similarly, Muddy Water Research previously alleged Eurofins had overstated their financials, which was found to be wrong after an independent audit by E&Y, where E&Y found “no material misstatements in its cash statements, refuting earlier allegations by short seller Muddy Waters.

We thus believe that these allegations are unlikely to carry any weight, as their data collection methods are not fully verifiable, and both short sellers have conflicts of interest, which incentivize them to publish short reports. Both these firms were also previously found to be wrong, indicating their report on the company can be inaccurate. In the event that the company was found to be utilizing unpermitted data collection methods, we believe this would reduce its ability to collect more data than its competitors and reduce AppDiscovery’s competitiveness in successfully targeting high-probability customers. This would lower its revenue growth, as its future competitiveness has weakened. In the event the company was found to have used unpermitted tracking devices, they also face potential financial fines by the SEC.

Based on Bloomberg, the largest financial penalty for AppLovin could potentially be $750 million, which accounts for 14% of 2026F net earnings. However, if the company were convicted of these allegations and were to pay the previous highest financial fine in FY2024, the costs would only be 2% of FY2026 net income, much lower than if they were in a class-action lawsuit. In the event of unlawful tracking, the SEC’s potential fines would lower net income for the company, particularly in our bear case.

Robust Growth Outlook

After determining that the company could potentially have weaker competitiveness and face potential financial costs, we analyze whether the company could continue to grow despite these competitive and regulatory concerns.

We see that its software platform has the highest growth rate (84.3%) among all its other segments and has grown 70.0% YoY in 2025. Its strong software platform growth can be mainly attributable to AXON’s architecture and its strong capabilities, such as its predictive analytics and reinforcement learning. As we previously highlighted, we indicated that as AXON gathers more data regarding users and users’ engagement, its ML model would improve over time, enabling AXON to better match “more relevant and higher-earning ads on a publisher's app.” As we indicated above, the company completed the sale of its Apps segment on June 30th, 2025, for $900 million, allowing the company to streamline its operations and focus on its software platform. As such, this reduced its reportable segments from 3 in 2024 to 1 for 2025.

To further analyze its software platform revenue, we compile the historical in-app advertising market and compare the market growth against its software platform growth.

From 2021 to 2025, its software platform growth has outpaced the market, growing on average 84.3%, whereas the market grew 20.5% on average. We believe this highlights its strong competitiveness in the market, whereby the company grew much quicker than the industry. Previously, in 2020, its software platform growth (4.5%) was much lower than the market (28.6%). However, in 2021, its software platform revenue grew tremendously by 225.1% YoY, whereas the market grew 38.8% YoY. In October 2020, the company released its AXON model, which utilized AI to deliver targeted advertising and improve ad engagement for advertisers. We believe that the newly released AXON has contributed to the strong platform growth in 2020, as AXON’s new AI capabilities improved ad engagement for its customers.

We overall determine that the company’s growth outlook remains robust, driven by its AXON architecture. We expect its architecture to improve in the long run, as it gathers more data from its AppDiscovery, MAX, and Adjust products, enabling more targeted advertising to users, thereby benefiting advertisers and app publishers' ad revenue. We further believe that the allegations by short sellers are unjustified, as their data collection methods and results are not fully verified by an independent research institution for digital advertising data, such as the Media Rating Council. These short-sellers have also previously been found to produce inaccurate reports, after an independent investigation by institutions. We thus update our revenue forecasts for the company.

In terms of our forecasts, we first calculate the growth factor of its software platform growth % YoY against the market growth % YoY to derive a factor of 5.45. We multiplied the forecasted growth of the in-app advertising market by the factor (5.45) to derive its growth for 2026F. We then taper down the company’s growth rate from 2026F to 2030F to discount future uncertainties that could impact its growth outlook, such as unexpected competitive developments and unexpected events.

Risk: Potential Regulatory Risk Impacting Competitiveness

We believe one key risk that could impact the company is regulatory risk. While we determined that the short-sellers' allegations have no justification, the SEC is currently investigating the company for identifier bridging and fingerprinting. If the company were to be convicted and found guilty of violating its platform partners’ service agreements, this could weaken AXON’s competitive advantage, as AXON’s access to data would be reduced, and AXON would be unable to effectively retarget and bid higher for customers with a higher probability of purchasing. As a result, the lower access to data would reduce AXON’s capabilities to serve more targeted advertising, thereby lowering its long-term competitiveness.

Valuation

Based on our valuation of the company using a DCF model, incorporating our revenue projections at a 5-year forward average growth of around 43.9% and a discount rate of 12.1%, reflecting the company’s WACC. For beta, we use the top application software companies' 5-year average beta of 1.29, as the company’s beta is abnormally high at 2.51. Using a terminal value based on a perpetual growth rate assumption on the long-term US growth of 2.45%, our model indicates an upside of 31.49%.

Verdict

We overall determine that the company’s growth outlook remains robust, driven by its AXON architecture, which consistently improves over time as its architecture gathers more data to better match ad supply and demand for advertisers and publishers. We also expect its large access to data through its MAX, AppDiscovery, Adjust, high mediation platform share, and high number of ad requests to sustain its long-term competitiveness. We further believe that the short-seller allegations have no justification, as these short-sellers’ digital data collection methods are not verified by a fully independent research institution, such as the Media Rating Council. These short-seller reports were also previously found to be inaccurate after thorough investigation by independent parties. Based on our updated DCF valuation, we rate the company as a Strong Buy with a price target of $662.38, which is higher than our previous target of $354.44, mainly due to higher revenue growth forecasts and better margins. We forecast the company to grow with a 10-year forward average growth of 28.49%, whereas we previously forecasted 19.22%, as the company sold off its App segment in 2025. We also projected its 10-year forward FCF margins as 57.36%, whereas we previously forecasted it as 28.41%, as the company streamlines its operations towards its software platform.

Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.

Wall Street ended lower on the week as elevated U.S.-Iran tensions remained in focus—with officials signaling the conflict could extend several more weeks—while President Donald Trump unveiled a high-profile tech advisory panel and Treasury yields surged to multi-month highs.

Geopolitical tensions between the U.S. and Iran remained elevated, with the conflict continuing to dominate market focus. Secretary of State Marco Rubio indicated to G7 foreign ministers that hostilities are expected to persist for another two to four weeks, suggesting the administration sees a potential end to the conflict within a relatively near-term window.

President Donald Trump has tapped several of the most prominent tech figures in business to serve on a technology advisory panel, including Meta Platforms (META) CEO Mark Zuckerberg, Oracle (ORCL) Executive Chairman Larry Ellison, and Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang, to help shape the administration’s approach to science and technology.

U.S. Treasury yields surged this week, with benchmark rates hitting multi-month highs and capturing global investor attention. The 10-year climbed to 4.48%—its highest since July 2025—while the 2-year and 30-year also reached their strongest levels in months, reflecting shifting expectations around monetary policy.

data.title: Klarna: Buy Now, Or Pay Later
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The previously highly valued private fintech company Klarna Group plc (KLAR) went public in 2025, and the initial market reaction could not be described as pleasant for pre-IPO shareholders. However, the significant plummet in Klarna's stock since its IPO presents an attractive risk-versus-reward case at the current valuation. The investment thesis consists of three core pillars, most of which the market currently ignores despite multiple data points already showing Klarna's ability to act on these opportunities.

Firstly, Klarna operates in 26 markets, representing $19 trillion in retail spending. Even when limiting the market potential to Klarna's already existing TAM, Klarna's take rate of 2.8% as of Q4 2025, implies $532 billion of potential revenue. This highlights how huge Klarna's TAM is, as only small market share increases are equivalent to explosive growth. Secondly, Klarna is on a mission to not only add new users but also to convert current basic BNPL customers to banking customers. Succeeding in transforming only a fraction of their 118 million active users would explode Klarna's ARPAC due to substantially higher revenue per banking user. Lastly, I believe Klarna has yet to show its true operating leverage, as the company will continue to implement AI in its mission to become even more efficient. Klarna's current AI implementation has already exploded revenue per employee by reducing headcount, which in theory funnels future revenue straight to the bottom line.

In addition to previous points, the market is currently punishing Klarna for what many would consider the result of a conservative accounting method, which heavily impacted Klarna's margins. As the market starts to view Klarna's path to profitability as more achievable and robust, as well as move past the recent accounting illusion depicted in Klarna's Q4, the stock should start its path towards IPO levels yet again.

Understanding the Business Model

Klarna's overall strategy could be divided into two separate phases. The first phase was the main focus for many years and implied a focus on scaling its global transaction network. This phase is now largely complete with 118 million active users and over one million merchants, allowing Klarna to focus more on its next phase. Klarna's substantial global transaction network, allows for its next phase of converting existing customers into full banking customers. This is done by offering credit cards, savings accounts, financing products, etc. Achieving success in Klarna's second phase would be extremely profitable for Klarna, as both revenue and margins per customer would experience explosive growth.

Making Money the Klarna Way

Although the market's belief that Klarna's revenue model is vulnerable to economic downturns is true, I believe the market doesn't give Klarna enough credit for being more diversified than its closest competitors. Klarna's revenue consists of three revenue streams: merchant, consumer, and advertising revenue.

  • The most significant revenue stream is merchant revenue, which derives from merchants paying Klarna fees for offering their customers flexible financing alternatives, leading to higher order value. This segment reached $743 million in Q4 2025, increasing by 24% from the year prior.
  • Secondly, consumer revenue represents a substantial part of Klarna's total revenue, although considerably less than for most competitors, including Affirm (AFRM). This segment includes interest income and is Klarna's fastest-growing revenue stream. As of Q4 2025, interest income increased by 47% to $267 million, driven by Fair Financing GMV increasing 165% YoY to $4.5 billion. However, this growth engine entails suppression of margins in the short-term, as accounting standards force Klarna to provision for credit losses upfront. In other words, explosive growth within this segment will indirectly cause a significant but temporary "artificial" impact on Klarna's margins.
  • Lastly, with 55 million MAU, Klarna monetizes its users through digital advertising and affiliate marketing. This represents a small part of Klarna's revenue and will most likely stay that way, but it is important to note that this value proposition only gets more valuable for merchants as Klarna's global network grows, hence having the potential to grow.

The Elephant in the Room

Klarna's recent Q4 results displayed strong revenue growth at 38% YoY, reaching $1 billion in quarterly revenue for the first time and beating its guidance. Furthermore, GMV increased 32% YoY to $38.7 billion, and active users exceeded 118 million, growing 28% from the year prior. In addition to this, Klarna reported promising numbers for its newer banking offering, with 15.8 million banking customers, representing an increase of 101% for the new and highly profitable segment.

However, this was unfortunately not enough to satisfy investors, as the market was more focused on Klarna's profit margins and, more specifically, a perceived miss on its transaction margin dollars (TMD). Klarna's TMD reached $622 million before provisions and $372 million after, compared to its guidance of $390 million, representing a 31% and 17% YoY increase, respectively. Since TMD displays Klarna's underlying credit performance, it acts as a core operating metric for Klarna. The market being alarmed by the miss in TMD would therefore be reasonable if it were not for a specific factor that makes these results misleading. Hence, the TMD before provisions number more accurately displays Klarna's true credit performance, which is far less concerning.

By following current accounting standards, Klarna is forced to book the provision for expected credit losses upfront when a loan is originated and only recognizes the interest revenue gradually over the loan's lifespan. This implies that every new loan Klarna originates, is unprofitable from an accounting view in the beginning and gradually becomes profitable as interest payments are paid. Since Klarna originated a massive volume of longer-term loans in Q4, this entailed a substantial upfront hit to margins, suppressing the underlying profitability of these loans. Hence, this miss could partly be explained as a result of Klarna managing to originate a significantly greater volume of loans.

Moreover, Klarna's credit performance shows stability as the charge-off rate for its riskier Fair Financing products fluctuates between 3% and 4%. Additionally, the blended average across all products is relatively low at 1.1% for banking and 0.6% for basic users. These numbers are closely aligned with standard industry averages, disproving the narrative of Klarna's credit quality deteriorating.

Path to Profitability

While the market seems concerned with Klarna's negative operating cash flow, it should only be viewed as natural for a growing bank with the mission to aggressively expand its loan book. Hence, the negative operating cash flow should not be perceived as alarming for Klarna's continued path towards profitability. Instead, multiple factors support Klarna achieving its long-term profitability potential.

Firstly, continued user base expansion and higher adoption of Klarna's banking offering would significantly boost revenue and margins. The metrics highlighted in the table below, clearly indicate the highly profitable conversion of transforming basic users into banking users. As of Q4 data, banking customers entail $107 in ARPU compared to the significantly lower $30 ARPU of a basic user. Hence, succeeding in continued adoption of Klarna's banking offering would have a substantial impact on Klarna's profitability.

The recent Q4 results already show great promise of converting users, demonstrating proof of their strategy. Despite growing 101% YoY, this segment remains relatively small at only 13% of the total user base. This speaks for continued potential to convert more users, and converting just an additional 10% of existing users would increase ARPU by around 25%. Moreover, this assumption ignores the potential of new banking products as well as continued user base expansion.

The explosive growth rate in active card users, consumer deposits, and banking consumers confirms that Klarna plays a bigger role for its users now than before. This supports Klarna's mission of becoming a more complete banking service for its customers. However, these deposits are also beneficial for another reason, since they provide Klarna with a lower-cost funding base compared to peers lacking the same structure. This enables wider net interest margins as the loan book continues to expand, which in turn contributes to the segment's profitability.

Lastly, Klarna's efficiency measures have already enabled the company to rapidly expand while simultaneously decreasing its total headcount, causing its revenue per employee to skyrocket by more than 250% since 2022 (see graph below). Additionally, adjusted operating expenses have decreased by 8% since 2022, while Klarna's revenue doubled, highlighting its impressive operating leverage. For 2026, management is guiding for at least 6.9% in adjusted operating margins, which should be more than achievable. Furthermore, once the hyper-growth within the Fair Financing products segment starts to slow down and stabilize, the deferred revenue will catch up with the upfront provisions, leading to rapid margin expansion.

Valuation

When comparing Klarna in relation to its closest competitor, Affirm, Klarna stands out as attractive. As displayed above, the revenue mix differs between the companies, as Affirm is more reliant on consumer fees and less diversified than Klarna. Consequently, Affirm is more sensitive to the cost of capital in the financial markets. This can be put in contrast to Klarna, which possesses an EU banking license and is experiencing rapidly growing consumer deposits. Therefore, Klarna should be more resilient to recessions and interest rate shocks, due to its revenue mix and more stable funding base. Although it is important to note that these superior characteristics are far from enough to change Klarna's sensitive nature to macroeconomic changes. Despite these advantages, Klarna currently trades at a discount to Affirm based on several forward-looking metrics, including EV/Sales.

However, Klarna's unique capital and business structure make it hard to conduct an effective peer valuation. Evaluating Klarna from a broader perspective, the stock seems cheap at a P/B ratio just under 2. This can be compared to US industry averages for banks of P/B at 1.62 and 3.66 for financial services excluding banks and insurance. This puts Klarna's P/B valuation above the industry average for banks, although below some banks such as JPMorgan. To value Klarna closely in line with banks growing at a CAGR around 4% is absurd, given Klarna could achieve growth north of 20%-25% for the next five years.

To further understand Klarna's potential within the next 5 years, three scenarios characterized by different growth, margins, and dilution are modeled in the table below. In a base case scenario, Klarna grows revenue at a 20% CAGR to $8.7 billion, with 12.5%, or $1.09 billion, reaching the bottom line. Assuming 12.5% dilution during the period and a reasonable P/E exit multiple of 20, it would imply a share price of $51 in 2030. There is a relatively probable outcome of Klarna exceeding these targets if they successfully execute converting users to banking users. Hence, in a bull case scenario, Klarna could achieve 25% CAGR and 15% net income margin, representing $10.7 billion in revenue and $1.6 billion in net income. In this case of higher profitability, Klarna would be able to counter a lot of stock-based compensation, leading to lower dilution. When assuming 10% dilution and a higher P/E multiple of 25 for this scenario, it equates to $96 a share in 2030. However, even when considering a bear case of 15% revenue CAGR, 10% net income margin, 15% dilution, and a 15x multiple, it's hard to imagine significant downside from here with an implied share price of $24. This implies substantial upside potential with a considerable margin of safety, as Klarna can miss these targets and still deliver substantial returns from current levels around $13.

Key Risks

However, there are three main risks investors should monitor closely that potentially could endanger the investment thesis:

  • Firstly, Klarna's dependency and exposure to credit risks make the company vulnerable to macroeconomic shifts. It's therefore crucial for Klarna and similar businesses to maintain sufficient risk management when originating loans. If a company fails to maintain sufficient risk management for its loan origination, a recession will quickly expose these vulnerabilities, or as the Buffett saying goes, "Only when the tide goes out do you discover who's been swimming naked".
  • Secondly, the finance sector is heavily scrutinized and regularly faces regulatory challenges, which is even more accurate for BNPL and consumer credit providers like Klarna. For instance, sudden changes in underwriting mandates can cause severe consequences for Klarna's Fair Financing segment. On the other hand, the regulatory landscape surrounding the finance market, also acts as a barrier to entry for new actors trying to enter the market.
  • Lastly, the rapidly growing $400 billion fintech market is not only good news for Klarna, as it attracts large and powerful competitors to the market. Hence, to survive within the competitive market that is fintech, companies need to be agile and aggressive. PayPal can be interpreted as a recent and clear case study on what can happen when a company fails to keep up with the competitive landscape of fintech. From a theoretical perspective, increased competition could increase Klarna's CAC and decrease its take rate. However, significant competition within the fintech market is nothing new, and Klarna's take rate has actually increased from 2.3% in 2022 to 2.7% for fiscal 2025. This shows Klarna's competitive strengths, as it historically has managed to successfully compete within the competitive sector.

The Bottom Line

Following its significant stock price plummet since going public, Klarna is finally at an intriguing valuation. The market is currently punishing Klarna for its Q4 transaction margin miss, although this miss merely represents the consequences of conservative accounting practices, driven by explosive growth in its Fair Financing segment.

Underneath all the noise is a dominant and highly efficient global payment network, with an impressive growth engine and operating leverage. With over 118 million active users and growing, the conversion of basic users to banking users remains a largely untapped and substantial growth opportunity for Klarna in the coming years.

These factors, combined with an attractive valuation, make Klarna one of the most compelling investment cases within the fintech sector as of today. Not surprisingly, I am not alone in this belief, as the CEO and co-founder increased his ownership to 7.49% just before the IPO. Moreover, Klarna Chairman Michael Moritz recently bought $50 million worth of shares following the stock's significant pullback, displaying considerable conviction among insiders.
